citation.authors: Fern, L., Draper, M., Oron-Gilad, T., Shively, R. J., Porat, T., Rottem-Hovev, M., & Silbiger, J.
citation.title: Multi-Operator Multi-UAV (MOMU) Control: Exploring the Influence of Sensor Tools and Playbook Task Delegation

extraction_metadata.notes: This article's three mission task types (marking civilians, tracking military targets, prosecuting HVTs) cleanly correspond to three distinct behavioral-relation gaps flagged this session (Search, Pursuit, and Engagement Task respectively) — a useful single-article confirmation of all three proposed codes at once. Re-coded 2026-07-10 against Task-Environment v0.8: all three now map onto the newly-defined Search Task (4.2), Pursuit Task (4.4), and Engagement Task (4.3) codes, replacing the original Unclear / Candidate New Code flags.

study_type.justification: A fully within-subjects, formally powered experiment (12 participants, 3x2x2 design, GLMM analysis of accuracy/reaction time/NASA-TLX) comparing three control modes is the article's sole contribution; MUSIM is an existing, previously-published testbed rather than a novel system introduced by this paper, so no Secondary code is needed.

summary.task: Pairs of participants (6 MOMU teams) shared a pool of 3 or 5 UAVs to conduct RSTA missions across two adjacent Areas of Operation (a high-resolution urban area with a single point of interest, and a lower-resolution suburban area along a route), each monitoring their assigned area for civilian and military vehicles and prioritizing four tasks: prosecuting High Value Targets (HVTs), tracking military vehicles, marking civilian vehicles, and responding to team chat messages, across six 8-minute scenarios per control-mode block.
summary.control: Three control modes were compared within-subjects: Manual (independently flying each UAV via point-click-drag waypoints and manually panning/tilting/zooming its sensor with a 6-DOF SpaceExplorer controller); Tools (three sensor-management aids — Line-of-Sight-with-Castling showing/predicting which UAV had the best view of a location, Maintain automatically holding a sensor's footprint size constant as the UAV moved, and Coupling linking multiple UAVs' cameras to a shared stare point); and Playbook (delegating high-level 'plays' — Surveillance, Route Recon, Prosecute Target — that automatically configured one or more UAVs' flight and sensor behavior to accomplish a task). UAV control could be requested and handed off between teammates, with only one operator able to actively control a given UAV at a time.
summary.participants: 12 male participants (ages 19-25, M = 21), organized into 6 pre-existing pairs (friends/co-workers), all frequent video-game players (>=10 hrs/week) with no military, piloting, or UAV experience, in a fully within-subjects 3 (Control: Manual/Tools/Playbook) x 2 (UAV Level: 3/5) x 2 (AO: urban/suburban) design analyzed with General Linear Mixed Models.
summary.results: Playbook and Manual produced significantly higher HVT prosecution accuracy than Tools (56-60% vs. 32%), and Playbook/Tools were nearly twice as fast as Manual for prosecuting HVTs in the urban AO (~33s vs. 65s). Playbook also produced significantly higher civilian-marking accuracy (66%) than Manual (58%) or Tools (60%), and significantly faster chat-response times, though Manual had a higher (but less accurate) chat response rate. Playbook and Manual scored significantly higher on the composite mission score (66% and 62%) than Tools (48%); five UAVs significantly improved the composite score over three. Subjective workload ratings showed no significant differences between control modes (all around 50% of scale), though both Tools and Plays were rated favorably for ease of use and mission usefulness.
summary.issues: The Tools condition consistently underperformed both Manual and Playbook on accuracy for the two highest-priority tasks (prosecuting HVTs, marking civilians), and participants rated Tools as less useful than Plays despite comparable ease-of-use ratings — likely because Tools' relevance to any given task was less immediately obvious and required more strategic learning to use well. Both Tools and Playbook produced a 'tunneling' effect, reducing response rates to the lower-priority chat task relative to Manual, though it is unclear whether this reflects a genuine attentional cost or simply participants' inexperience with the new aids. Playbook's benefit was explicitly tied to how well its three pre-built plays matched the specific tasks tested; the authors caution this may not generalize to more dynamic environments where available plays are a poor match to the situation.
summary.recommendations: Continue developing task-specific automation 'plays' for supervisory control since their direct relevance to concrete tasks made value obvious to operators and drove the strongest performance; ensure the play library can be modified/adapted for more dynamic or unpredictable tasking environments. Improve the intuitiveness and task-specific framing of generic sensor-management Tools and provide dedicated training on when and how to use them. Investigate the workload/performance tradeoff of adding more UAV assets further, since performance improved with 5 UAVs over 3 while subjective workload stayed manageable in both conditions, suggesting room to add further resources.
summary.autonomous_capabilities: Playbook automation could autonomously fly and sensor-point one or more UAVs to accomplish a delegated task: a Surveillance play autonomously loitered and pre-pointed assigned UAVs' sensors at points of interest; a Route Recon play dynamically generated a flight path around a designated route and autonomously slewed the assigned UAVs' sensors along it; and a Prosecute Target play autonomously locked assigned UAVs' sensors onto a selected target and staged the laser/weapon in standby, leaving only final engagement (lase, then fire) to the operator. Independently of Playbook, an autotrack function could autonomously keep a UAV's sensor centered on a moving target until the target stopped, at which point it automatically disengaged, and a Coupling tool could autonomously slave one or more UAVs' cameras to follow another UAV's camera movements.

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: Civilian and military vehicles (30 civilian, 6-8 military, 2-4 HVT per scenario) are multiple separate instances that arise intermittently from a point of interest or along a route.
Vehicles drive out of the POI or along the route and continue moving until stopped, requiring the operator to continuously track them rather than address them once.

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: Marking civilian vehicles as friendly is a detection/identification relation, not avoidance — matching Section 4.2 (Search Task), added in v0.8.

Tracking a military vehicle with autotrack until it stops or reveals a weapon is continuous custody-maintenance of an already-identified moving target — matching Section 4.4 (Pursuit Task), added in v0.8.
Prosecuting a confirmed HVT (lase and fire) is a goal-directed engagement relation where contact with the target is the objective — matching Section 4.3 (Engagement Task), added in v0.8.

team_size.justification: Two operators per team share a pool of 3 or 5 UAVs.

team_composition.justification: All UAVs are notional, identical fixed-wing vehicles with fixed airspeed/altitude across all conditions.
Both operators have identical roles, capabilities, and interface access, alternating which AO they are assigned to across trials.

interaction_model.justification: In Manual/Tools modes, an operator's commands address one specific UAV they currently control at a time.
A single Playbook play can configure the flight and sensor behavior of multiple assigned UAVs at once.

interaction_directness.justification: Operators issue explicit, addressed commands to specific UAVs or plays via the interface.

communication_proximity.justification: Operators interact with UAVs entirely through networked Ground Control Stations, with no physical co-location with the simulated vehicles.
The article does not state whether the two teamed operators were physically co-located in the same room or seated at separated, only network-linked stations.

coordination_architecture.justification: No coordination or negotiation mechanism among the UAVs themselves is described; each UAV independently executes its assigned loiter/route/play, with the two human operators as the sole coordinating agents (via request/handoff of individual vehicles).

computational_characteristics.justification: Playbook could optionally choose which of the participant's currently-controlled UAVs to assign to a called play, a task-allocation decision distinct from any single UAV's own motion computation.

The autotrack function keeps the sensor centered on a tracked vehicle via image-based tracking, a deterministic geometric/visual-servoing computation rather than a named learned or population-based method.

The Route Recon play's flight-path generation is described only as 'dynamically generated... based on the location of the assigned UAVs and the route,' without naming a specific path-planning algorithm family.

human_involvement_level.justification: Manual waypoint assignment and sensor steering (in both Manual and Tools modes) require continuous operator input to operate as intended.
Once a Playbook play is called or autotrack is engaged, flight/sensor positioning or target tracking proceeds without further operator input; the operator monitors and can intervene.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: Manual sensor control via the 6-DOF SpaceExplorer continuously and directly sets the gimbal's pan/tilt/zoom.
Both manual waypoint/flight-path assignment and calling a Playbook play are discrete goal-level inputs, with the system executing the necessary steps to reach them.

operational_domain.justification: All vehicles are fixed-wing UAVs operating in the air domain.

flags.note: No robot-to-robot coordination mechanism is described; coordination resides entirely with the two human operators.
No specific path-computation algorithm named for the Route Recon play.
Physical co-location of the two teamed operators relative to each other is not stated.
